Framework for implementation of the guidelines 47
5. It is not currently known whether a programme based on education, diagnosis and 
treatment is adequate to produce the maximum benefit in a country.  In general, it is believed that these essential baseline services should be supplemented by a population-based screening programme for breast cancer, though several of the details of such a programme are controversial, and subject to ongoing research. These  guidelines are based on the current understanding of the evidence, coupled with a recognition that, in many of the countries in the Region, it is not currently practicable to introduce a population-based mammography screening programme.  For a country that has the resources to undertake such a programme, the policy recommendations of an IARC Working Group (2002) [21] should be followed.
6. In the absence of the resources required to initiate a population-based mammography 
screening programme, a programme comprising physical (clinical) examinations of the breast combined with the teaching of breast self-examination should be considered.  Such a programme should either be introduced initially as a demonstration project in one area, and only extended to other areas when it is fully functional in the initial area, or arrangements should be made through the Regional Office for the country to participate in one of the ongoing screening trials of these modalities to help collect  
evidence on their effectiveness.
7. If a country decides to initiate a demonstration project in population-based screening 
by clinical breast  examination combined with the teaching of breast self-examination, it should be planned so that its effectiveness can be fully evaluated.  In particular, potential problems in recruitment of women to the programme, the administration of the tests, the referral of women with abnormalities for diagnosis and ensuring adequate treatment of those diagnosed with breast cancer, will have to be fully evaluated in relation to the health care system of the country, and, if necessary, solutions found.
8. The age group of women participating in programmes requires careful consideration.  
Because of the population pyramid in the countries of the Region, the proportion of breast cancers diagnosed under the age of 50 is greater than in industrialized countries, though in terms of the rates of breast cancer in different age groups, the rates at younger ages are not higher than in industrialized countries. Further, the evidence, even for mammography screening, is of lower efficacy at ages 40–49 than over the age of 50, while there is no evidence of efficacy under the age of 40.  
Hence, caution is advised in terms of the age group included, and in most countries it will be preferable to concentrate on women age 40–69.
9. Screening clinical breast examinations should only be performed by specially 
trained health professionals, either female physicians or nurses. Many texts and 

48 Guidelines for the early detection and screening of breast cancer
some publications provide details on the recommended techniques [23]. Sufficient 
time should be allowed in arranging appointments to provide the time for a careful, skilled examination to be performed. As it is desirable that breast self-examination is individually taught at the same time as the breast physical examination, 15 minutes for each appointment is recommended.
10. Experience within and outside the Region has confirmed that only an organized 
screening programme, as recommended by IARC [21], will be effective. Thus there has to be: a means to identify the target population; a mechanism to invite women comprising the target population to attend for screening; a mechanism in place to ensure that those women with a detected abnormality do attend for diagnosis, and if confirmed to have breast cancer, treatment; a mechanism to ensure that women offered treatment complete the treatment courses recommended; a mechanism to follow up such women; and a mechanism to invite women screened negative to return for re-screening at the appropriate time.  These components of an organised system require a carefully designed information system. Such a system should be capable of producing the data necessary to evaluate the effectiveness of the programme.
11. It is currently not known whether it is necessary to repeat screening by breast physical 
examinations on an annual basis. If resources permit, annual re-screening of the target group is believed to be ideal, but screening approximately every 18 months to 2 years may be resorted to if the resources are such that annual screening cannot be offered.

Consensus opinion of the regional task force 
for developing breast cancer prevention,  
screening and management guidelines
The task force reviewed all available scientific literature on the subject including 
the findings of the Global Summit Early Detection Panel,1 which address early detection 
of breast cancer in countries with limited resources. The following conclusions and recommendations were made. 
Conclusions
 The Eastern Mediterranean Region has a huge area between its borders with an 
ethnically diverse population, and significant differences in health care facilities and accessibility. 
 It is important to have accurate and updated census data, as well as data on cancer in 
general and breast cancer in particular, including age-specific incidence and mortality data. 
 These recommendations should be carefully studied and a set of guidelines developed 
that reflect them all. Guidelines that are applicable to one country, may not be applicable in another, therefore the feasibility and cost-effectiveness of guidelines should be carefully studied.
 There are three commonly-used diagnostic tools for breast cancer screening in 
resource-rich societies. These include mammography, clinical breast examination and breast self-examination.
 There is a key distinction between early detection and screening. Early detection is 
the identification of breast cancer at a stage in its natural history where the impact of therapy has the greatest chance of producing a cure. It may include programmes by which breast cancer that is causing symptoms can be diagnosed at an earlier stage. By comparison, screening for a disease means using a test on individuals or populations without any signs or symptoms of the disease of interest in order to identify those who are most likely to have the condition. It is a public health strategy that involves 
1 Anderson BO et al. Early detection of breast cancer in countries with limited resources. The Breast Journal , 
2003, 9(2):S51–S59.

Annex 2 53
administrating these tests to well, asymptomatic population groups. The purpose of 
screening is to identify a subgroup that warrants further evaluation. It is important to understand and carefully address false positive and false negative tests. A screening mammography is one tool for improving early detection.
 Because screening can take many forms, it is important to be clear about the forms 
under consideration. The task force reviewed screening evidence and balanced it against the available resources using the WHO framework. A distinction should be made between opportunistic screening and organized screening. Opportunistic screening occurs when a health care provider refers an individual who has no symptoms of breast cancer for a screening test. If the screening test shows an abnormality, then the woman and the provider take responsibility for coordinating and completing the diagnostic evaluation of that abnormality. Organized screening occurs as part of a programme that coordinates within a specific community, encouraging women to obtain a screening test at set time intervals. The screening programme must have the resources to perform a screening test and a complete diagnostic evaluation on those women who have abnormal results in their screening test.
Recommendations
Breast cancer screening in countries with limited resources
 The task force acknowledged the WHO approach of not advocating breast cancer 
screening in countries with low or medium level resources, based on the rationale that resources in such countries need to be reserved for cancer diagnosis and treatment, and possibly for screening of cervical cancer. In contrast, the task force supports the Global Summit Early Detection Panel, which suggests that screening can be implemented in countries with limited resources within a centralized cancer facility at which breast cancer treatment is available. Such programmes will not cover the country’s entire population and will only provide care to the portion of the population that has access to the facility. This recommendation is based on the premise that institution-based screening could be a pilot programme for more extensive programmes covering larger populations, and ultimately the entire population as more resources become available.
Early detection programmes without formal screening
 The task force supports and endorses the promotion of public education and awareness 
about breast cancer in most countries, regardless of their resource level, but recognizes that these efforts need to occur hand-in-hand with the establishment of diagnosis and treatment programmes. 

54 Guidelines for the early detection and screening of breast cancer
Approach to education
 The task force supports the rights of all women to be educated about breast cancer. 
This education should be culturally appropriate and targeted towards, and tailored to, individual populations so that it can have the greatest impact. The points that should be stressed in public education programmes should include: 
– breast cancer can kill.
– breast cancer can be effectively treated if detected early and need not kill.– the signs and symptoms of breast cancer.– most breast lumps are not cancer.– breast cancer is diagnosed by biopsy rather than mastectomy.
Breast self-examination
 Breast self-examination screening should be distinguished from breast health 
awareness. Health education activities could promote breast health awareness by urging women to become familiar with the feel and the appearance of their breast and to seek medical evaluation if they notice change in their breasts. There is hardly any evidence to support the promotion of the breast self-examination approach. Self-examination is not associated with increased detection rates or any reduction in breast cancer-specific mortality. In fact, in a Chinese randomized study of 13 000 healthy women there was an increased incidence of anxiety and surgical procedures with increased costs from unnecessary secondary investigations. However, we do not have evidence about the impact of breast self-examination in countries where women have greater breast cancer awareness and typically present with late stage breast cancer.
2
Clinical breast examination 
 Clinical breast examination is a standardized procedure whereby a health care provider 
examines a woman’s breast, chest wall and axilla. Like mammography, clinical breast examination can be either a screening test or a diagnostic test depending on the clinical setting in which it is applied. It is the consensus of the task force that clinical breast examination should be part of routine health examinations, whether used as a screening test in women who do not examine themselves, or as a diagnostic test in women who have found a change themselves. This practice will increase breast health awareness in both the women and the health care provider. Clinical breast examination has general qualities that suggest benefit through its routine use. The available data suggest that clinical breast examination does not have much benefit in women who are already being screened by mammography. The task force agrees that clinical breast examination alone is not recommended for screening as 
2 Ibid.

Annex 2 55
a substitute for mammography. For countries that do not have the resources for a 
mammography, the use of clinical breast examination alone for screening might be a potentially interesting research topic. 
Screening mammography
 Mammography is the only screening modality that has evidence from randomized 
trials showing reduced mortality in women who were offered screening. The strongest evidence supporting the efficacy of screening mammography is for women aged 50–74 years. Evidence also supports the value of mammography for women aged 40 –49 years and although mammography is less cost-effective in this age group, the potential for mortality reduction is similar to that observed in older women. In the United States, the Department of Health and Human Services has issued a statement formally recommending that all women aged 40 and older undergo screening mammography at 1–2 year intervals. There is no data to support its use in women under the age of 40.
